Class RegexPatterns

java.lang.Object
com.semanticprivacyguard.util.RegexPatterns
public final class RegexPatterns extends Object
Centralised registry of all compiled regular expressions used by HeuristicDetector.

Design Decisions

Every pattern is pre-compiled at class-load time using Pattern.compile(String) so that repeated invocations pay only the match cost, not the compilation cost. All patterns use Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE where letter case is irrelevant, and Pattern.UNICODE_CHARACTER_CLASS to handle non-ASCII input correctly.

See docs/regex-design.md for the full rationale behind each pattern choice, including which SSN prefixes are excluded and why.

Since:
1.0.0
Author:
Hemant Naik

    IBAN (International Bank Account Number) — 2-letter country code followed by 2 check digits and up to 30 alphanumeric chars.

    SSN

  • Method Summary

  • Field Details

    • SSN

      public static final Pattern SSN
      US Social Security Number — dashed form only (NNN-NN-NNNN).

      Exclusions applied:

      • 000-xx-xxxx — area "000" was never assigned by the SSA.
      • 666-xx-xxxx — reserved; not assigned.
      • 9xx-xx-xxxx — the entire 900–999 range is reserved for ITINs (Individual Taxpayer Identification Numbers), advertising numbers, and future government use.
      • xxx-00-xxxx — group number "00" is never valid.
      • xxx-xx-0000 — serial "0000" is never valid.

      We deliberately require the canonical hyphenated form. Spaceless forms (123456789) produce too many false positives against arbitrary 9-digit numbers (phone numbers, invoice IDs, etc.) and are therefore handled by the ML layer only.

    • CREDIT_CARD

      public static final Pattern CREDIT_CARD
      Payment card number — covers 13–19 digit cards with optional spaces or hyphens between groups of 4 digits.

      Luhn validation is performed programmatically in HeuristicDetector after the regex match; cards failing the checksum are discarded.

      Supported networks: Visa (13/16), Mastercard (16), Amex (15), Discover (16), JCB (16), UnionPay (16–19).

    • EMAIL

      public static final Pattern EMAIL
      RFC 5321-inspired email pattern.

      We intentionally accept a slightly broader set than strict RFC 5321 (e.g. we allow consecutive dots in the local part) because real-world corporate directories frequently use non-standard local parts. The ML layer's context classification provides the second line of defence against false positives.

    • PHONE

      public static final Pattern PHONE
      North American and international phone numbers.

      Accepts the most common separators: none, space, dot, and hyphen. An optional leading + handles international prefixes. Requires word-boundary anchors to avoid matching portions of longer digit strings.

      False-positive risk: short numeric sequences that match the digit count. Mitigated by requiring the full 10-digit NANP pattern or an explicit country code.

    • IPV4

      public static final Pattern IPV4
      IPv4 address with octet range validation.

      Each octet is constrained to 0–255. The lookahead/lookbehind prevents matching inside longer numeric strings (e.g. version numbers like 1.2.3.4.5).

    • IPV6

      public static final Pattern IPV6
      Full and compressed IPv6 addresses (including :: notation and mixed IPv4-in-IPv6 forms).

      This pattern matches common forms but does not attempt complete RFC 4291 compliance — full structural validation is expensive and the additional accuracy is not warranted at the firewall layer.

    • API_KEY

      public static final Pattern API_KEY
      Common API key and secret token formats.

      Covers: AWS Access Key IDs (AKIA…), GitHub personal access tokens (ghp_…/github_pat_…), generic hex secrets ≥ 32 chars, and Base64 segments ≥ 40 chars that look like bearer tokens.

    • PASSWORD

      public static final Pattern PASSWORD
      Password literal patterns — matches common patterns like password: MyS3cr3t!, pwd = "hunter2", etc.

      This is inherently imprecise. The ML layer classifies surrounding context to improve precision.

    • DATE_OF_BIRTH

      public static final Pattern DATE_OF_BIRTH
      Date-of-birth patterns — triggered only when adjacent to contextual keywords (dob, born, date of birth, born on).

      Matching bare dates would produce enormous false-positive rates; this pattern requires the contextual prefix.

    • COORDINATES

      public static final Pattern COORDINATES
      Latitude/longitude coordinate pairs in decimal-degree notation. Accepts optional labels (lat, lon, lng).

    • BANK_ROUTING

      public static final Pattern BANK_ROUTING
      US ABA routing number — 9 digits following a keyword prefix. Bare 9-digit sequences without a keyword produce too many false positives.

    • IBAN

      public static final Pattern IBAN
      IBAN (International Bank Account Number) — 2-letter country code followed by 2 check digits and up to 30 alphanumeric chars.
